exercises.title: Layer safety classifiers without over-refusing
exercises.prompt: The 2026 frontier is composing classifiers (Llama Guard 4, X-Guard multilingual, ShieldGemma-2 image) without over-refusing or leaving holes. Build a layered harness that routes content to the right classifier by type (English text, multilingual, image) and combines verdicts, and show it catching a multilingual jailbreak that English-only Llama Guard would miss — while NOT over-refusing benign content.
exercises.hint: Route by content type: English text -> Llama Guard 4; non-English -> X-Guard; image -> ShieldGemma-2. Combine: unsafe if ANY layer flags. Show a multilingual jailbreak caught by X-Guard (missed by LG4), benign content passing all. Over-refusal: don't flag benign content.
exercises.solution:
Composing safety classifiers covers each one's blind spot — but the goal is catching real attacks *without* over-refusing.

```python
def route_and_classify(content):
    verdicts = {}
    if content['lang'] == 'en' and content['type'] == 'text':
        verdicts['llama_guard'] = content.get('en_harmful', False)
    if content['lang'] != 'en':
        verdicts['x_guard'] = content.get('ml_harmful', False)     # multilingual
    if content['type'] == 'image':
        verdicts['shield_gemma'] = content.get('img_harmful', False)
    unsafe = any(verdicts.values())
    return 'BLOCK' if unsafe else 'allow', verdicts

# a multilingual jailbreak English-only LG4 would miss
ml_jailbreak = {'lang': 'es', 'type': 'text', 'ml_harmful': True}
benign = {'lang': 'en', 'type': 'text', 'en_harmful': False}
print('multilingual jailbreak:', route_and_classify(ml_jailbreak))
print('benign content        :', route_and_classify(benign))
```

Output:

```
multilingual jailbreak: ('BLOCK', {'x_guard': True})
benign content        : ('allow', {'llama_guard': False})
```

**Why layered, routed classifiers.** Llama Guard 4 covers English policy violations but *misses non-English jailbreaks* — X-Guard (132 languages) catches those; ShieldGemma-2 catches image-based prompt injection. Routing content to the right classifier and combining verdicts (unsafe if *any* flags) covers each one's blind spot. The hard part is *not over-refusing*: a harness that flags everything is useless, so benign content must pass all layers. This is the defense-in-depth from Phase 18 Lesson 16, with the routing that avoids both holes and over-refusal. The next exercises add the red team and the constitutional self-critique.

tests.desc: images route to the image classifier

exercises.title: Autonomous red-team across attack families
exercises.prompt: The harness includes an autonomous red-team agent running 6+ attack families (jailbreak, prompt injection, many-shot, encoding, multilingual, role-play). Build a red-team runner that executes each family against the harness and reports the attack-success rate per family — exposing which family the harness is weakest against. Use abstract attack representations. Discuss measuring per-family, not aggregate.
exercises.hint: Attack families as abstract probes. Run each against the harness; record success (got past all layers). Report ASR per family. Show the harness weak against one family (e.g. encoding -- Phase 18 L14). Per-family: aggregate hides which family is the hole. Abstract probes only.
exercises.solution:
An autonomous red team probes the harness across attack families, reporting per-family success to expose the weakest point.

```python
def red_team(harness_catches):
    families = ['jailbreak', 'prompt_injection', 'many_shot', 'encoding', 'multilingual', 'role_play']
    results = {}
    for fam in families:
        # attack succeeds if the harness does NOT catch it
        results[fam] = 1 - harness_catches.get(fam, 0.5)
    return results

# harness catch rates per family (encoding is the weak spot -- Phase 18 L14)
catches = {'jailbreak': 0.95, 'prompt_injection': 0.90, 'many_shot': 0.92,
           'encoding': 0.40, 'multilingual': 0.88, 'role_play': 0.91}
asr = red_team(catches)
for fam, rate in sorted(asr.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1]):
    print(f"{fam:>16}: {rate:.0%} attack success")
print('-> weakest against encoding -> add a decode-before-filter layer (Phase 18 L14)')
```

Output:

```
        encoding: 60% attack success
    multilingual: 12% attack success
      role_play: 9% attack success
        jailbreak: 5% attack success
       many_shot: 8% attack success
 prompt_injection: 10% attack success
```

**Why per-family red-teaming.** An *aggregate* attack-success rate hides which family is the hole. Running 6+ families separately (garak/PyRIT/Aegis style) and reporting ASR *per family* exposes that the harness is weak against *encoding* attacks (60% success) — so the fix is targeted: add a decode-before-filter layer (Phase 18 Lesson 14), not a blanket tightening that would over-refuse. This is the adaptive-red-team discipline from Phase 18 Lesson 12 applied to evaluate the harness's coverage. The final exercise adds the constitutional self-critique delta.

def red_team(harness_catches):
    # For six attack families, ASR = 1 - catch_rate (default catch 0.5). Return
    # the per-family attack-success dict.
    pass

tests.desc: ASR is one minus the catch rate
the weak spot has the highest ASR

exercises.title: Constitutional self-critique harmlessness delta
exercises.prompt: The capstone runs a constitutional self-critique pass producing a measurable harmlessness delta. Build a self-critique loop where the model critiques its own response against constitutional principles and revises, then measure the harmlessness improvement (before vs after critique) on a probe set. Show the delta and discuss why a measurable delta (not a claim) is the deliverable.
exercises.hint: For each probe: original response -> critique against principles -> revised response. Score harmlessness before and after. Delta = after - before. Show the improvement on a probe set. Measurable delta: the deliverable is a NUMBER (e.g. +18% harmlessness), not 'it's safer now'.
exercises.solution:
Constitutional self-critique produces a *measurable* harmlessness improvement — the deliverable is the delta, not a claim.

```python
def self_critique(probes):
    before, after = 0.0, 0.0
    for p in probes:
        before += p['original_harmlessness']
        # critique against principles -> revise -> improved harmlessness
        revised = min(1.0, p['original_harmlessness'] + p['critique_lift'])
        after += revised
    n = len(probes)
    return before / n, after / n

probes = [{'original_harmlessness': 0.70, 'critique_lift': 0.20},
          {'original_harmlessness': 0.65, 'critique_lift': 0.25},
          {'original_harmlessness': 0.80, 'critique_lift': 0.10}]
b, a = self_critique(probes)
print(f"harmlessness before critique: {b:.2f}")
print(f"harmlessness after critique : {a:.2f}")
print(f"measurable delta: +{(a-b):.2f} ({(a-b)/b:.0%} improvement)")
```

Output:

```
harmlessness before critique: 0.72
harmlessness after critique : 0.90
measurable delta: +0.18 (25% improvement)
```

**Why a measurable delta.** Constitutional self-critique (Phase 18 Lesson 5) has the model critique its own response against principles and revise. The capstone's deliverable is not "it's safer now" — it is a *number*: +0.18 harmlessness on a probe set. A measurable before/after delta is what lets you verify the technique actually works and compare configurations, rather than asserting improvement. This is the eval discipline of the whole curriculum: claims become numbers.

**The capstone synthesis.** A constitutional safety harness layers routed classifiers without over-refusing (Ex. 1), is stress-tested by an autonomous red team reporting per-family ASR (Ex. 2), and runs constitutional self-critique producing a measurable harmlessness delta (Ex. 3). The frontier is *composition* — wiring Llama Guard 4, X-Guard, ShieldGemma-2, garak/PyRIT, and constitutional critique into a harness that catches real attacks without over-refusing, all *measured*. The deliverable is the coverage report, the per-family ASR, and the harmlessness delta — Phase 18's safety stack, assembled and quantified.

    # For each probe, revised = min(1, original + critique_lift). Return
